**Digital distribution**, also referred to as **content delivery**, **online distribution**, or **electronic software distribution**, among others, is the delivery or distribution of information or materials through digital platforms.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The distribution of [digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media") content may be of digitized versions of analog materials, as well as other materials offered in a purely digital format, such as [audio](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction"), [video](/wiki/Motion_graphics "Motion graphics"), [e-books](/wiki/E-books "E-books"), [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"), and other [software](/wiki/Software "Software").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The term is generally used to describe distribution over an online delivery medium, such as the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), thus bypassing physical distribution methods, such as [paper](/wiki/Paper "Paper"), [optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc"), and [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") videocassettes. The term online distribution is typically applied to freestanding products, with downloadable add-ons for other products are more commonly described as [downloadable content](/wiki/Downloadable_content "Downloadable content"). Content distributed online may be streamed or downloaded, and often consists of [books](/wiki/Book "Book"), [films](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television programs](/wiki/Television_program "Television program"), [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), software, and video games. Streaming involves downloading and using content at a user's request, or "on-demand", rather than allowing a user to store it permanently. In contrast, fully downloading content to a [hard drive](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive") or other forms of storage media may allow offline access in the future.

Specialist networks known as [content delivery networks](/wiki/Content_delivery_network "Content delivery network") help distribute content over the Internet by ensuring both high availability and high performance.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Alternative technologies for content delivery include [peer-to-peer file sharing](/wiki/Peer-to-peer_file_sharing "Peer-to-peer file sharing") technologies. Alternatively, [content delivery platforms](/wiki/Content_delivery_platform "Content delivery platform") create and syndicate content remotely, acting like hosted [content management systems](/wiki/Content_management_system "Content management system").

Unrelated to the above, the term "digital distribution" is also used in [film distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") to describe the distribution of content through physical digital media, in opposition to distribution by analog media such as [photographic film](/wiki/Photographic_film "Photographic film") and [magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape "Magnetic tape") (see: [digital cinema](/wiki/Digital_cinema "Digital cinema")).

## Impact on traditional retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Impact on traditional retail")]

The rise of online distribution has provided controversy for the traditional business models and resulted in challenges as well as new opportunities for traditional retailers and publishers. Online distribution affects all of the traditional media markets, including music, press, and broadcasting. In Britain, the [iPlayer](/wiki/IPlayer "IPlayer"), a software application for streaming television and radio, accounts for 5% of all bandwidth used in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[4]](#cite_note-KEA-4)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Music")]

The move towards online distribution led to a dip in sales in the 2000s; [CD](/wiki/CD "CD") sales were nearly cut in half around this time.[[5]](#cite_note-5) One such example of online distribution taking its toll on a retailer is the Canadian music chain [Sam the Record Man](/wiki/Sam_the_Record_Man "Sam the Record Man"); the company blamed online distribution for having to close a number of its traditional retail venues in 2007–08.[[6]](#cite_note-6) One main reason that sales took such a big hit was that unlicensed downloads of music were very accessible.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With copyright infringement affecting sales, the music industry realized it needed to change its business model to keep up with the rapidly changing technology.[[7]](#cite_note-Janssens-7) The step that was taken to move the music industry into the online space has been successful for several reasons. The development of lossy audio compression file formats such as [MP3](/wiki/MP3 "MP3") could take 30 MB for a typical 3-minute song and bring it down to 3 MB without any serious loss of quality.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Lossless [FLAC](/wiki/FLAC "FLAC") files can be up to six times larger than an MP3 while,[[9]](#cite_note-9) in comparison, the same song might require 30–40 megabytes of storage on a CD.[[7]](#cite_note-Janssens-7) The smaller file size yields much greater Internet transfer speeds.

The transition into the online space has boosted sales, and profit for some artists.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It has also allowed for potentially lower expenses such as lower coordination costs, lower distribution costs, as well as the possibility for redistributed total profits.[[7]](#cite_note-Janssens-7) These lower costs have aided new artists in breaking onto the scene and gaining recognition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the past, some emerging artists have struggled to find a way to market themselves and compete in the various distribution channels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Internet may give artists more control over their music in terms of ownership, rights, creative process, pricing, and more. In addition to providing global users with easier access to content, online stores allow users to choose the songs they wish instead of having to purchase an entire album from which there may only be one or two titles that the buyer enjoys.

The number of downloaded single tracks rose from 160 million in 2004 to 795 million in 2006, which accounted for a revenue boost from US$397 million to US$2 billion.[[7]](#cite_note-Janssens-7) Downloading peaked in the US in 2012, after which it started falling due to the rise of [music streaming services](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service"). In 2017, physical formats overtook downloading again for the first time in six years,[[11]](#cite_note-11) but despite the [vinyl revival](/wiki/Vinyl_revival "Vinyl revival") and CDs holding its own, the physical formats account for only 11% revenue as of 2023, while streaming services are dominant with 84% of the US industry.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Videos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Videos")]

With the advancement of network bandwidth capabilities, online distribution became prominent in the 21st century, with prominent platforms such as [Amazon Video](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), and [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix")'s [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") service starting in 2007.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Many traditional network television shows, movies and other video content is now available online, either from the content owner directly or from third-party services. [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"), [Vudu](/wiki/Vudu "Vudu"), [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"), [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV"), [SlingTV](/wiki/Sling_TV "Sling TV") and other Internet-based video services allow content owners to let users access their content on computers, smartphones, tablets or by using appliances such as video game consoles, set-top boxes or [Smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Many film distributors also include a [Digital Copy](/wiki/Digital_copy "Digital copy"), also called Digital HD, with [Blu-ray disc](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), [Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray"), [Blu-ray 3D](/wiki/Blu-ray#Blu-ray_3D "Blu-ray") or a [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD").

### Books

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Books")]

Some companies, such as Bookmasters Distribution, which invested US$4.5 million in upgrading its equipment and operating systems, have had to direct capital toward keeping up with the changes in technology.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The phenomenon of books going digital has given users the ability to access their books on handheld digital book readers. One benefit of [electronic book readers](/wiki/E-reader "E-reader") is that they allow users to access additional content via hypertext links. These electronic book readers also give users portability for their books since a reader can hold multiple books depending on the size of its [hard drive](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive").[[15]](#cite_note-MacInnes-15) Companies that are able to adapt and make changes to capitalize on the digital media market have seen sales surge. Vice President of [Perseus Books Group](/wiki/Perseus_Books_Group "Perseus Books Group") stated that since shifting to electronic books (e-books), it saw sales rise by 68%[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. [Independent Publishers Group](/wiki/Independent_Publishers_Group "Independent Publishers Group") experienced a sales boost of 23% in the first quarter of 2012 alone.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Tor Books](/wiki/Tor_Books "Tor Books"), a major publisher of science fiction and fantasy books, started to sell [e-books](/wiki/E-book "E-book") [DRM-free](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") by July 2012.[[17]](#cite_note-17) One year later the publisher stated that they will keep this model as removing DRM was not hurting their digital distribution ebook business.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Smaller e-book publishers such as [O'Reilly Media](/wiki/O%27Reilly_Media "O'Reilly Media"), [Carina Press](/wiki/Carina_Press "Carina Press")[[19]](#cite_note-19) and [Baen Books](/wiki/Baen_Books "Baen Books") had already forgone DRM previously.

### Video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Video games")]

Main article: [Digital distribution of video games](/wiki/Digital_distribution_of_video_games "Digital distribution of video games")

Online distribution is changing the structure of the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Gabe Newell](/wiki/Gabe_Newell "Gabe Newell"), creator of the digital distribution service [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"), formulated the advantages over physical [retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail") distribution as such:

> The worst days [for game development] were the cartridge days for the NES. It was a huge risk – you had all this money tied up in silicon in a warehouse somewhere, and so you'd be conservative in the decisions you felt you could make, very conservative in the IPs you signed, your art direction would not change, and so on. Now it's the opposite extreme: we can put something up on Steam, deliver it to people all around the world, make changes. We can take more interesting risks.[...] Retail doesn't know how to deal with those games. On Steam [a digital distributor] there's no shelf-space restriction.

— [Gabe Newell](/wiki/Gabe_Newell "Gabe Newell"), *[Rock, Paper, Shotgun](/wiki/Rock,_Paper,_Shotgun "Rock, Paper, Shotgun")* (2007)[[20]](#cite_note-newell2007-20)

Since the 2000s, there has been an increasing number of smaller and niche titles available and commercially successful, e.g. [remakes](/wiki/Remake_(software) "Remake (software)") of classic games.[[21]](#cite_note-monkeyislandrerelease-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The new possibility of the digital distribution stimulated also the creation of game titles of very small video game producers like [Independent game developer](/wiki/Independent_game_developer "Independent game developer")[[23]](#cite_note-garr11-23)[[24]](#cite_note-stuart10-24) and [Modders](/wiki/Mod_(video_gaming) "Mod (video gaming)") (e.g. [Garry's Mod](/wiki/Garry%27s_Mod "Garry's Mod")[[25]](#cite_note-garrysmod-25)), which were before not commercially feasible.

The years after 2004 saw the rise of many digital distribution services on the PC, such as [Amazon Services](/wiki/Amazon_Digital_Services "Amazon Digital Services"), [Desura](/wiki/Desura "Desura"), [GameStop](/wiki/GameStop "GameStop"), [Games for Windows – Live](/wiki/Games_for_Windows_%E2%80%93_Live "Games for Windows – Live"), [Impulse](/wiki/Impulse_(software) "Impulse (software)"), [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"), [Origin](/wiki/Origin_(service) "Origin (service)"), [Battle.net](/wiki/Battle.net "Battle.net"), [Direct2Drive](/wiki/Direct2Drive "Direct2Drive"), [GOG.com](/wiki/GOG.com "GOG.com"), [Epic Games Store](/wiki/Epic_Games_Store "Epic Games Store") and [GamersGate](/wiki/GamersGate "GamersGate"). The offered properties differ significantly: while most of these digital distributors do not allow reselling of bought games, *[Green Man Gaming](/wiki/Green_Man_Gaming "Green Man Gaming")* allows this. Another example is *[gog.com](/wiki/Gog.com "Gog.com")* which has a strict [non-DRM](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") policy[[26]](#cite_note-arspreview-26) while most other services allow various (strict or less strict) forms of DRM.

Digital distribution is also more eco-friendly than physical. Optical discs are made of [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate") plastic and [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminum "Aluminum"). The creation of 30 of them requires the use of 300 cubic feet of [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), two cups of oil and 24 gallons of water.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The protective cases for an optical disc is made from [polyvinyl chloride](/wiki/Polyvinyl_chloride "Polyvinyl chloride") (PVC), a chemical that is dangerous to produce, [vinyl chloride monomer](/wiki/Vinyl_chloride_monomer "Vinyl chloride monomer") being a known [carcinogen](/wiki/Carcinogen "Carcinogen").[[27]](#cite_note-CW-27)

## Challenges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Digital_distribution&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Challenges")]

A general issue is the large number of incompatible data formats in which content is delivered, possibly restricting the devices that may be used, or making data conversion necessary. [Streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") services can have several drawbacks: requiring a constant Internet connection to use content; the restriction of some content to never be stored locally; the restriction of content from being transferred to [physical media](/wiki/Physical_media "Physical media"); and the enabling of greater censorship at the discretion of owners of content, infrastructure,[[28]](#cite_note-28) and consumer devices.

Decades after the launch of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"), in 2019 businesses were still adapting to the evolving world of distributing content digitally—even regarding the definition and understanding of basic terminology.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
